channel.title: Orthodox Christian Sermons: 4th Sunday of Luke
channel.description: Audio Orthodox Christian Sermons: 4th Sunday of Luke

item.title: 4th Sunday of Luke, 2011-10-30
item.description: :4th Sunday of Luke. The parable of the sower is a rare parable, in that it is explained by the Lord Himself. This explanation is actually longer than most people think and we discuss what His other words, which are not part of the traditional reading, mean. These include "No man, when he hath lighted a candle, covereth it with a vessel" and "Take heed therefore how ye hear: for whosoever hath, to him shall be given; and whosoever hath not, from him shall be taken even that which he seemeth to have." Together with the parable, the explanation teaches us the basic purpose of life and how to be "good ground".
item.itunes:author: Priest Seraphim Holland
item.itunes:summary: :4th Sunday of Luke. The parable of the sower is a rare parable, in that it is explained by the Lord Himself. This explanation is actually longer than most people think and we discuss what His other words, which are not part of the traditional reading, mean. These include "No man, when he hath lighted a candle, covereth it with a vessel" and "Take heed therefore how ye hear: for whosoever hath, to him shall be given; and whosoever hath not, from him shall be taken even that which he seemeth to have." Together with the parable, the explanation teaches us the basic purpose of life and how to be "good ground".

item.title: 4th Sunday of Luke, 2013-10-27
item.description: :4th Sunday of Luke. The most important part of the Parable of the Sower is the Good Ground. We must be that good ground if we are to be saved. How? The parable does little to explain. The Epistle today explains: "He which soweth sparingly shall reap also sparingly; and he which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully". This is the key to happiness in this life and the next.
item.itunes:author: Priest Seraphim Holland
item.itunes:summary: :4th Sunday of Luke. The most important part of the Parable of the Sower is the Good Ground. We must be that good ground if we are to be saved. How? The parable does little to explain. The Epistle today explains: "He which soweth sparingly shall reap also sparingly; and he which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully". This is the key to happiness in this life and the next.

item.title: 4th Sunday of Luke, Parable of the Sower, 2014-10-26
item.description: :4th Sunday of Luke. The Parable of the Sower and the Seed is one of the few parables explained by Christ, but still needs to be talked about because many have no idea what this parable means, nor its explanation. The purpose of Christianity is fulfilled only by bearing fruit. This parable describes 4 kinds of ground, that is, souls, and only one kind bears fruit and is saved. We look quickly at the 3 kinds of ground that are not saved, especially focusing on the words that should be emphasized, such as "cares and riches and pleasures". The description of the good ground is the most important part of the parable, and we look at the critical words "honest and good heart" This parable refutes the heresy of "faith without works", but more importantly, teaches us what we must do to prepare our ground to receive the seed and be filled with God. the parable does not tell us that we must stop sinning to be saved, but must have an honest and good heart. This is good news for those who desire to be saved!
item.itunes:author: Priest Seraphim Holland
item.itunes:summary: :4th Sunday of Luke. The Parable of the Sower and the Seed is one of the few parables explained by Christ, but still needs to be talked about because many have no idea what this parable means, nor its explanation. The purpose of Christianity is fulfilled only by bearing fruit. This parable describes 4 kinds of ground, that is, souls, and only one kind bears fruit and is saved. We look quickly at the 3 kinds of ground that are not saved, especially focusing on the words that should be emphasized, such as "cares and riches and pleasures". The description of the good ground is the most important part of the parable, and we look at the critical words "honest and good heart" This parable refutes the heresy of "faith without works", but more importantly, teaches us what we must do to prepare our ground to receive the seed and be filled with God. the parable does not tell us that we must stop sinning to be saved, but must have an honest and good heart. This is good news for those who desire to be saved!
